title: Welcome to my website!
text:
Facebook
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Email
  




Welcome to my website!
I am Lily (Shi-tao) Zhang who is trying to be a good bio-statistician.
My interest in biology began after reading a biography of Jane Goodall. Her lifelong dedication to understanding chimpanzee behavior deeply inspired me and motivated me to pursue a path in biological research.
After earning my PhD in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, I came to the United States to continue developing my research skills. Through my training, I became increasingly drawn to working with data-especially the process of uncovering patterns and generating insights from complex data sets. During my PhD, I developed strong interests in coding and statistical analysis, and I found that this data-driven approach was where I could make the most meaningful impact. This experience ultimately led me to pursue further training in bio-statistics.
On this website, you will find my published works, and also find news and updates on my current research projects. I invite you to explore my website and learn more about me. Please feel free to contact me if you have any questions or would like to discuss potential projects.

title: Projects
text: I have worked with diverse real-world data sets in public health, clinical trials, and aging research. Across these projects, I applied R and SAS for data cleaning, statistical modeling, causal inference, and clear communication of results.

title: Projects
section: Mental Health and Disability in Florida
text:
Mental Health and Disability in Florida
Data: 2024 BRFSS (Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System)
Tools: R, Quarto
Focus
Examined mental health burden (number of poor mental health days) and disability status among adults in Florida using survey data.
Key takeaway
Lower socioeconomic status, especially lower income, was strongly associated with worse mental health outcomes and higher disability risk.
Methods used
Linear and logistic regression, variable recoding, survey-style data processing

title: Projects
section: Treatment Effect Estimation in Heart Failure
text:
Treatment Effect Estimation in Heart Failure
Data: Digitalis Investigation Group trial
Tools: R
Focus
Estimated treatment effects on worsening heart failure using causal inference approaches.
Key takeaway
Results were consistent across unadjusted and adjusted analyses, reflecting the randomized design, while highlighting the importance of balance diagnostics in propensity score methods.
Methods used
Propensity score matching, weighting (ATT), balance assessment (SMD), Causual inference

title: Projects
text:
Diabetes Treatment and Glycemic Control
Data: NHANES 2017–2020
Tools: R
Focus
Compared treatment patterns and glycemic control (HbA1c) among adults with type 2 diabetes using national survey data.
Key takeaway
Observed treatment differences are influenced by patient characteristics and access to care, emphasizing the need for careful interpretation in observational studies.
Methods used
Data integration across files, exposure definition, regression modeling

title: Projects
section: Health and Chronic Disease in Older Adults
text:
Health and Chronic Disease in Older Adults
Data: RAND HRS (Health and Retirement Study), 2022
Tools: R
Focus
Analyzed self-rated health and chronic disease burden in older adults.
Key takeaway
Health status in aging populations is multi-dimensional and requires careful variable construction and modeling strategies.
Methods used
Ordinal and count outcome modeling, variable derivation, missing data handling

title: Projects
section: Healthcare Utilization and Injury Patterns After a Hurricane
text:
Healthcare Utilization and Injury Patterns After a Hurricane
Data: HCUP State Emergency Department Database (SEDD), Florida 2018 Tools: SAS, R
Focus
Analyzed emergency department (ED) visits following Hurricane Michael to characterize injury patterns and identify factors associated with patient disposition.
Key takeaway
Abrasions/contusions were the most common injuries, while older age and more severe conditions (e.g., fractures, mental health diagnoses) significantly increased the likelihood of hospital transfer. Insurance type also reflected differences in healthcare utilization, with Medicare patients more likely to require higher levels of care.
Methods used
Large-scale administrative data cleaning, ICD-10-based outcome definition, stratified analysis, multinomial logistic regression

title: Projects
text:
County-Level Public Health Modeling
Data: County Health Rankings
Tools: R
Focus
Modeled associations between public health factors and unemployment at the county level.
Key takeaway
Community-level health indicators can be linked to broader socioeconomic outcomes, though interpretation requires careful model checking.
Methods used
Count models, regression diagnostics, data visualization

title: Projects
text:
Technical Skills Highlight

Programming: R, SAS

Modeling: Linear, logistic, count models, survival analysis

Causal Inference: Propensity score matching and weighting

Data Types: Survey data, clinical trial data, longitudinal and public data sets

Reproducibility: Quarto reports and data workflows


This portfolio reflects my interest in applying statistical methods to real-world health data and translating results into meaningful insights.

title: Publications
text:
Paper Link
Human dipeptidyl-peptidase III (hDPP III) is capable of specifically cleaving dipeptides from the N-terminal of small peptides with biological activity such as angiotensin II (Ang II, DRVYIHPF), and participates in blood pressure regulation, pain modulation, and the development of cancers in human biological activities. In this study, 500 ns molecular dynamics simulations were performed on free-hDPP III (PDB code: 5E33), hDPP III-Ang II (PDB code: 5E2Q), and hDPP III-IVYPW (PDB code: 5E3C) to explore how these two peptides affect the catalytic efficiency of enzymes in terms of the binding mode and the conformational changes. Our results indicate that in the case of the hDPP III-Ang II complex, subsite S1 became small and hydrophobic, which might be propitious for the nucleophile to attack the substrate. The structures of the most stable conformations of the three systems revealed that Arg421-Lys423 could form an α-helix with the presence of Ang II, but only part of the α-helix was produced in hDPP III-IVYPW. As the hinge structure in hDPP III, the conformational changes that took place in the Arg421-Lys423 residue could lead to the changes in the shape and space of the catalytic subsites, which might allow water to function as a nucleophile to attack the substrate. Our results may provide new clues to enable the design of new inhibitors for hDPP III in the future.

title: Publications
text:
Paper Link
Human dipeptidyl-peptidase III (hDPP III) is capable of specifically cleaving dipeptides from the N-terminal of small peptides with biological activity such as angiotensin II (Ang II, DRVYIHPF), and participates in blood pressure regulation, pain modulation, and the development of cancers in human biological activities. In this study, 500 ns molecular dynamics simulations were performed on free-hDPP III (PDB code: 5E33), hDPP III-Ang II (PDB code: 5E2Q), and hDPP III-IVYPW (PDB code: 5E3C) to explore how these two peptides affect the catalytic efficiency of enzymes in terms of the binding mode and the conformational changes. Our results indicate that in the case of the hDPP III-Ang II complex, subsite S1 became small and hydrophobic, which might be propitious for the nucleophile to attack the substrate. The structures of the most stable conformations of the three systems revealed that Arg421-Lys423 could form an α-helix with the presence of Ang II, but only part of the α-helix was produced in hDPP III-IVYPW. As the hinge structure in hDPP III, the conformational changes that took place in the Arg421-Lys423 residue could lead to the changes in the shape and space of the catalytic subsites, which might allow water to function as a nucleophile to attack the substrate. Our results may provide new clues to enable the design of new inhibitors for hDPP III in the future.

title: Publications
section: Molecular Danymic simulation of N-Terminal Human Maltase-Glucoamylase
text:
Molecular Danymic simulation of N-Terminal Human Maltase-Glucoamylase

Paper Link
There are multiple drugs for the treatment of type 2 diabetes, including traditional sulfonylureas biguanides, glinides, thiazolidinediones, α-glucosidase inhibitors, glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists, dipeptidyl peptidase IV (DPP-4) inhibitors, and sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors. α-Glucosidase inhibitors have been used to control postprandial glucose levels caused by type 2 diabetes since 1990. α-Glucosidases are rather crucial in the human metabolic system and are principally found in families 13 and 31. Maltase-glucoamylase (MGAM) belongs to glycoside hydrolase family 31. The main function of MGAM is to digest terminal starch products left after the enzymatic action of α-amylase; hence, MGAM becomes an efficient drug target for insulin resistance. In order to explore the conformational changes in the active pocket and unbinding pathway for NtMGAM, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and adaptive steered molecular dynamics (ASMD) simulations were performed for two NtMGAM-inhibitor [de-O-sulfonated kotalanol (DSK) and acarbose] complexes. MD simulations indicated that DSK bound to NtMGAM may influence two domains (inserted loop 1 and inserted loop 2) by interfering with the spiralization of residue 497–499. The flexibility of inserted loop 1 and inserted loop 2 can influence the volume of the active pocket of NtMGAM, which can affect the binding progress for DSK to NtMGAM. ASMD simulations showed that compared to acarbose, DSK escaped from NtMGAM easily with lower energy. Asp542 is an important residue on the bottleneck of the active pocket of NtMGAM and could generate hydrogen bonds with DSK continuously. Our theoretical results may provide some useful clues for designing new α-glucosidase inhibitors to treat type 2 diabetes.

title: Check out my new blog here!
text:
In the book ‘How to Be a Modern Scientist’, there is a chapter that emphasizes the importance of communicating with others who work in the same field. Communication plays a crucial role in scientific research, as one can always learn from others regardless of differences in academic degree or career stage.
The book highlights two main ways to engage in such communication. One approach is to share work before a paper is formally published, for example by posting it on a preprint server. This allows researchers to receive meaningful comments and constructive advice from others in the field. While this process may also invite criticism, it can spark innovative ideas and lead to improvements that would not have emerged otherwise.
Another approach is to communicate after the paper is published, such as by writing a blog post or sharing the work on social media. This not only helps expand the reach and impact of the research, but also allows researchers to build their professional identity and increase visibility within the scientific community.
Science is not done in a vacuum. Great ideas often arise from the exchange of perspectives and constructive debate. That said, it is equally important not to be overly influenced by negativity on the internet. Scientists are often independent thinkers with strong opinions and a high sense of pride, and learning how to engage thoughtfully while maintaining confidence is an essential skill in modern scientific practice.
